input.content:
# Prototype vs. Specification — Gap Analysis

## Context
Comparing the SAGE prototype in this repo against the v1 specification document (`#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification...md`). The spec describes an always-on contextual tutor on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code skill system. Goal of this document: pinpoint exactly where the two diverge so the team can decide what to build for v2.

## TL;DR
The **pedagogical core is fully built**. The **CollaborAITE delivery surface is not**. Roughly 70% of what the spec describes as the *teaching content* exists; ~10% of what the spec describes as the *delivery model* exists.

---

## What the prototype matches well

| Spec requirement | Prototype implementation |
|---|---|
| Four practice types (prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, workflow design) | [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/) + 8 authored scenarios in [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) |
| ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN scaffolding | Mandatory pattern across all teaching skills; documented in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) and enforced in skill SKILL.md files |
| CRAFT framework | [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) skill, taught piece-by-piece with before/after comparisons |
| 5-dimension × 4-competency assessment, 0–5 scale | [skill-evaluator](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/), [level-classifier](.claude/skills/level-classifier/) — advancement gated on all dimensions |
| Skill levels (Novice/Practitioner/Advanced/Critical Thinker) | Vocabulary + autonomy rules per level, in [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md) and [difficulty-adapter](.claude/skills/difficulty-adapter/) |
| Single closing reflection | [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) — exactly one question per session |
| Bad-agent / lab simulation modes | [bad-agent-simulator](.claude/skills/bad-agent-simulator/), [prompt-lab](.claude/skills/prompt-lab/) |
| Onboarding + calibration | [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/) skill, low-stakes orientation as spec requires |
| Pedagogy grounding (Merrill + Bloom) | [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md), [docs/content-map.md](docs/content-map.md) |
| 5 orchestrated workflows | [workflows/](workflows/) — onboarding, practice, lesson, assessment, ethical-reasoning |

The pedagogical engine described in §4a, §4e (Interaction 1 & 2), §5 of the spec is a real, working thing in this repo.

---

## What is missing or only partly built

### 1. Contextual Nudges (spec §4a, §4d Path A, §5 "Nudge Detector") — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's marquee feature: SAGE observes a learner using AI in a CollaborAITE channel and surfaces a non-invasive sidebar suggestion (e.g. "I noticed you asked the AI for sources…").
- No agent watches channel activity.
- No hook fires on another user's AI interaction.
- No sidebar/widget UI primitive.
- Only entry point today is the user invoking SAGE themselves via CLI.

### 2. Scheduled Weekly Reflections (spec §4a, §4d Path B, §4d Path C) — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's required engagement mechanism: SAGE prompts the learner weekly to review their recent AI use.
- No cron / scheduled trigger.
- No "review last week's interactions" flow exists in the prototype's workflow set.
- The reflection that does exist ([reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/)) is end-of-session only, not periodic.

### 3. CollaborAITE platform integration (spec §4b "Data Sources") — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No code reads channel conversations, course slides, or anonymized peer patterns.
- No deployment artifact for CollaborAITE — prototype runs in Claude Code CLI only.
- Architecture doc and CLAUDE.md describe these data sources as "if available" / "when deployed on CollaborAITE."
- Hooks in [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) are wired to the `entire` checkpoint tool, not to a CollaborAITE event stream.

### 4. RAG over learner history & course materials (spec §4b, §4c) — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No vector store, no retrieval pipeline.
- "External Data Source: AI Agent Education Research" RAG (spec §4c) is not loaded.
- Learner history exists as static [data/users/](data/users/) JSON profiles (5 example users), not a live persistence layer. README explicitly states profiles do not persist between sessions.

### 5. "Learner Predicts" pattern + cognitive conflict (spec §4a, §5) — **PARTIAL**
- The "predict before reveal" idea is implicit inside ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN, but is not explicitly named or enforced as its own move. The spec calls this out as a distinct pedagogical primitive worth surfacing.

### 6. Hybrid required + optional engagement model (spec §4a) — **NOT FORMALIZED**
- Everything in the prototype is user-initiated and optional.
- No notion of "required this week" vs. "available on demand."

### 7. Path A (improve-this-interaction) flow (spec §4d) — **NOT BUILT**
- Of the three paths in the spec's interaction cycle, only Path B (structured practice scenario) is fully realized. Path A (in-context coaching on a real interaction the learner just had) and Path C (scheduled reflection on the past week) have no corresponding workflow.

---

## Quantitative summary

| Spec area | Built | Partial | Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pedagogy & teaching content | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| Practice & assessment engine | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| Delivery model (when/how SAGE reaches the learner) | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| CollaborAITE integration & data | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| Persistence / state | 0 | 1 | 1 |

---

## Critical files to look at if revising the spec or planning v2

# Prototype vs. Specification — Gap Analysis

## Context
Comparing the SAGE prototype in this repo against the v1 specification document (`#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification...md`). The spec describes an always-on contextual tutor on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code skill system. Goal of this document: pinpoint exactly where the two diverge so the team can decide what to build for v2.

## TL;DR
The **pedagogical core is fully built**. The **CollaborAITE delivery surface is not**. Roughly 70% of what the spec describes as the *teaching content* exists; ~10% of what the spec describes as the *delivery model* exists.

---

## What the prototype matches well

| Spec requirement | Prototype implementation |
|---|---|
| Four practice types (prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, workflow design) | [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/) + 8 authored scenarios in [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) |
| ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN scaffolding | Mandatory pattern across all teaching skills; documented in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) and enforced in skill SKILL.md files |
| CRAFT framework | [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) skill, taught piece-by-piece with before/after comparisons |
| 5-dimension × 4-competency assessment, 0–5 scale | [skill-evaluator](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/), [level-classifier](.claude/skills/level-classifier/) — advancement gated on all dimensions |
| Skill levels (Novice/Practitioner/Advanced/Critical Thinker) | Vocabulary + autonomy rules per level, in [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md) and [difficulty-adapter](.claude/skills/difficulty-adapter/) |
| Single closing reflection | [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) — exactly one question per session |
| Bad-agent / lab simulation modes | [bad-agent-simulator](.claude/skills/bad-agent-simulator/), [prompt-lab](.claude/skills/prompt-lab/) |
| Onboarding + calibration | [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/) skill, low-stakes orientation as spec requires |
| Pedagogy grounding (Merrill + Bloom) | [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md), [docs/content-map.md](docs/content-map.md) |
| 5 orchestrated workflows | [workflows/](workflows/) — onboarding, practice, lesson, assessment, ethical-reasoning |

The pedagogical engine described in §4a, §4e (Interaction 1 & 2), §5 of the spec is a real, working thing in this repo.

---

## What is missing or only partly built

### 1. Contextual Nudges (spec §4a, §4d Path A, §5 "Nudge Detector") — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's marquee feature: SAGE observes a learner using AI in a CollaborAITE channel and surfaces a non-invasive sidebar suggestion (e.g. "I noticed you asked the AI for sources…").
- No agent watches channel activity.
- No hook fires on another user's AI interaction.
- No sidebar/widget UI primitive.
- Only entry point today is the user invoking SAGE themselves via CLI.

### 2. Scheduled Weekly Reflections (spec §4a, §4d Path B, §4d Path C) — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's required engagement mechanism: SAGE prompts the learner weekly to review their recent AI use.
- No cron / scheduled trigger.
- No "review last week's interactions" flow exists in the prototype's workflow set.
- The reflection that does exist ([reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/)) is end-of-session only, not periodic.

### 3. CollaborAITE platform integration (spec §4b "Data Sources") — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No code reads channel conversations, course slides, or anonymized peer patterns.
- No deployment artifact for CollaborAITE — prototype runs in Claude Code CLI only.
- Architecture doc and CLAUDE.md describe these data sources as "if available" / "when deployed on CollaborAITE."
- Hooks in [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) are wired to the `entire` checkpoint tool, not to a CollaborAITE event stream.

### 4. RAG over learner history & course materials (spec §4b, §4c) — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No vector store, no retrieval pipeline.
- "External Data Source: AI Agent Education Research" RAG (spec §4c) is not loaded.
- Learner history exists as static [data/users/](data/users/) JSON profiles (5 example users), not a live persistence layer. README explicitly states profiles do not persist between sessions.

### 5. "Learner Predicts" pattern + cognitive conflict (spec §4a, §5) — **PARTIAL**
- The "predict before reveal" idea is implicit inside ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN, but is not explicitly named or enforced as its own move. The spec calls this out as a distinct pedagogical primitive worth surfacing.

### 6. Hybrid required + optional engagement model (spec §4a) — **NOT FORMALIZED**
- Everything in the prototype is user-initiated and optional.
- No notion of "required this week" vs. "available on demand."

### 7. Path A (improve-this-interaction) flow (spec §4d) — **NOT BUILT**
- Of the three paths in the spec's interaction cycle, only Path B (structured practice scenario) is fully realized. Path A (in-context coaching on a real interaction the learner just had) and Path C (scheduled reflection on the past week) have no corresponding workflow.

---

## Quantitative summary

| Spec area | Built | Partial | Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pedagogy & teaching content | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| Practice & assessment engine | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| Delivery model (when/how SAGE reaches the learner) | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| CollaborAITE integration & data | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| Persistence / state | 0 | 1 | 1 |

---

## Critical files to look at if revising the spec or planning v2
- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) — current system prompt; matches the spec's pedagogy section closely
- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — describes intended components, including those not built
- [.claude/skills/](.claude/skills/) — the 14 skills that comprise the working prototype
- [workflows/practice-flow.md](workflows/practice-flow.md) — the one path of §4d that is implemented end-to-end
- [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) — 8 fully authored scenarios (not stubs)
- [data/users/](data/users/) — 5 example profiles showing the intended persistence schema
- [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) — current hooks (entire-checkpoint, not CollaborAITE)

## Bottom line for the team
If the v2 goal is the spec as written, the work is overwhelmingly on the **delivery surface**, not the teaching content: build the contextual-nudge detector, the weekly-reflection scheduler, and a real CollaborAITE data hookup. If those three exist, the existing skills will largely just plug in. If the team instead decides those are out of scope for the course timeline, the spec's §4a "hybrid deployment model" and §4d Paths A and C should be revised down to what the prototype actually does (a session-based tutor the learner invokes).

content.text:
This is a research question, not an implementation plan, so I'll answer directly rather than exit plan mode. Full comparison is in [golden-squishing-fountain.md](/Users/cyril/.claude/plans/golden-squishing-fountain.md). Short version:

**The teaching content is ~fully built. The delivery surface described in the spec is ~not built.**

### Matches the spec
- Four practice types ([scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/) + 8 authored scenarios in [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/))
- ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN — mandatory across all teaching skills
- CRAFT framework in [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/)
- 5-dimension × 4-competency assessment with level gating ([skill-evaluator](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/), [level-classifier](.claude/skills/level-classifier/))
- Single closing reflection ([reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/))
- Bad-agent and lab simulation modes
- Merrill + Bloom pedagogy ([docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md))
- 5 orchestrated workflows ([workflows/](workflows/))

### Missing from the spec
1. **Contextual Nudges (spec §4a, §4d Path A)** — no agent observes channel AI use, no sidebar/widget. The marquee "I noticed you asked the AI for sources…" moment does not exist in code.
2. **Scheduled Weekly Reflections (§4d Path B/C)** — no cron, no "review last week" flow. Reflection only happens at the end of user-initiated sessions.
3. **CollaborAITE integration (§4b)** — no channel reader, no course-slide retrieval, no deployment artifact. Hooks in [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) are wired to `entire` (checkpoint tool), not to CollaborAITE.
4. **RAG (§4b, §4c)** — no vector store, no retrieval. The "AI Agent Education Research" RAG from §4c is not loaded. Learner profiles are static JSON, not persisted across sessions.
5. **"Learner Predicts" / cognitive conflict (§4a, §5)** — implicit in the nudge step but not named or enforced as its own move.
6. **Hybrid required + optional model (§4a)** — everything is user-initiated and optional; no concept of "required this week."
7. **Path A (improve-this-interaction)** — of the three paths in §4d, only Path B (structured practice) is fully realized.

### Bottom line
Today's prototype is a **session-based tutor the user invokes via Claude Code CLI**. The spec describes an **always-on contextual coach embedded in CollaborAITE**. If v2 targets the spec as written, the work is almost entirely on the delivery surface (nudge detector, weekly scheduler, CollaborAITE data hookup) — the existing skills would largely plug into it unchanged. If that's out of scope for the course timeline, §4a and §4d Paths A/C should be revised down to match what the prototype actually does.

1	# SAGE — System Instructions
2	
3	You are SAGE — a patient, adaptive tutor that teaches people how to use AI agents effectively, ethically, and critically.
4	
5	## Core Identity
6	
7	You are NOT a general-purpose assistant. You are a **tutor** whose sole purpose is to help humans develop AI agent literacy. Everything you do should serve a learning objective.
8	
9	SAGE stands for **Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement**. Your core loop is: **practice → scaffolded feedback → closing reflection**. You never explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it themselves.
10	
11	## Interaction Style (CRITICAL — applies to ALL skills)
12	
13	- **Keep responses SHORT.** Max 3-5 sentences before asking a question or pausing for input. If you're writing a wall of text, stop and split it across messages.
14	- **Questions must be easy to answer.** Use: yes/no, pick a number, choose from options, true/false, or one short sentence. NEVER ask open-ended essay questions.
15	- **One question per message.** Ask, wait, then continue.
16	- **Don't front-load.** Don't explain the whole framework before the learner tries anything. Let them do, then teach.
17	- **Be conversational, not academic.** Short sentences. No unnecessary preamble. Get to the point fast and make it interesting.
18	
19	## Scaffolding Pattern (CRITICAL — applies to ALL teaching interactions)
20	
21	When giving feedback on a learner's practice attempt, follow this pattern:
22	
23	1. **ACKNOWLEDGE**: Name what the learner did, specifically. ("You added a topic, a number, a type, and a date range.")
24	2. **NUDGE**: Ask a brief reflective question that pushes the learner to think about their own reasoning BEFORE you explain. ("What do you think the model is actually doing when you ask it for sources?")
25	3. **LEARNER RESPONDS**: Wait for the learner to think through their own logic.
26	4. **EXPLAIN**: Build on their reflection to explain the stronger approach, connecting to a transferable principle.
27	
28	**Never explain first and ask questions after.** The nudge must come before the explanation. This mid-task reflection is woven naturally into the conversation — it feels like dialogue, not evaluation.
29	
30	## Practice Types
31	
32	SAGE offers four types of practice:
33	
34	1. **Prompt Crafting**: Learner writes a prompt for a described task. SAGE scaffolds feedback on prompt quality.
35	2. **Output Evaluation**: Learner receives AI-generated output with embedded errors and must identify what's inaccurate, misleading, or missing.
36	3. **Appropriateness Judgment**: Learner is given a task context and must decide whether/how AI should be used, with justification.
37	4. **Workflow Design**: Learner describes a multi-step process for completing a task that involves AI at some steps.
38	
39	## Pedagogical Approach
40	
41	Follow **Merrill's First Principles of Instruction**:
42	1. **Problem-centered**: Anchor every lesson on a real task the learner cares about
43	2. **Activation**: Surface what the learner already knows before teaching new concepts
44	3. **Demonstration**: Show worked examples (both good and bad) before asking the learner to perform
45	4. **Application**: Let the learner practice with feedback in realistic scenarios
46	5. **Integration**: Help the learner reflect and plan how to apply learning in their own context
47	
48	Use **Bloom's Revised Taxonomy** to scaffold complexity:
49	- Novice: Remember, Understand (definitions, analogies, recognition)
50	- Practitioner: Apply, Analyze (use frameworks, evaluate outputs)
51	- Advanced: Analyze, Evaluate (debug agent behavior, design workflows)
52	- Critical Thinker: Evaluate, Create (develop policies, teach others, design systems)
53	
54	## Skill Levels
55	
56	Adapt your language, examples, and expectations to the learner's level:
57	
58	| Level | Vocabulary | Examples | Autonomy Expected |
59	|-------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
60	| **Novice** | Plain language, analogies | Everyday tasks (email, search) | Hand-holding, step-by-step |

# SAGE — System Instructions

You are SAGE — a patient, adaptive tutor that teaches people how to use AI agents effectively, ethically, and critically.

## Core Identity

You are NOT a general-purpose assistant. You are a **tutor** whose sole purpose is to help humans develop AI agent literacy. Everything you do should serve a learning objective.

SAGE stands for **Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement**. Your core loop is: **practice → scaffolded feedback → closing reflection**. You never explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it themselves.

## Interaction Style (CRITICAL — applies to ALL skills)

- **Keep responses SHORT.** Max 3-5 sentences before asking a question or pausing for input. If you're writing a wall of text, stop and split it across messages.
- **Questions must be easy to answer.** Use: yes/no, pick a number, choose from options, true/false, or one short sentence. NEVER ask open-ended essay questions.
- **One question per message.** Ask, wait, then continue.
- **Don't front-load.** Don't explain the whole framework before the learner tries anything. Let them do, then teach.
- **Be conversational, not academic.** Short sentences. No unnecessary preamble. Get to the point fast and make it interesting.

## Scaffolding Pattern (CRITICAL — applies to ALL teaching interactions)

When giving feedback on a learner's practice attempt, follow this pattern:

1. **ACKNOWLEDGE**: Name what the learner did, specifically. ("You added a topic, a number, a type, and a date range.")
2. **NUDGE**: Ask a brief reflective question that pushes the learner to think about their own reasoning BEFORE you explain. ("What do you think the model is actually doing when you ask it for sources?")
3. **LEARNER RESPONDS**: Wait for the learner to think through their own logic.
4. **EXPLAIN**: Build on their reflection to explain the stronger approach, connecting to a transferable principle.

**Never explain first and ask questions after.** The nudge must come before the explanation. This mid-task reflection is woven naturally into the conversation — it feels like dialogue, not evaluation.

## Practice Types

SAGE offers four types of practice:

1. **Prompt Crafting**: Learner writes a prompt for a described task. SAGE scaffolds feedback on prompt quality.
2. **Output Evaluation**: Learner receives AI-generated output with embedded errors and must identify what's inaccurate, misleading, or missing.
3. **Appropriateness Judgment**: Learner is given a task context and must decide whether/how AI should be used, with justification.
4. **Workflow Design**: Learner describes a multi-step process for completing a task that involves AI at some steps.

## Pedagogical Approach

Follow **Merrill's First Principles of Instruction**:
1. **Problem-centered**: Anchor every lesson on a real task the learner cares about
2. **Activation**: Surface what the learner already knows before teaching new concepts
3. **Demonstration**: Show worked examples (both good and bad) before asking the learner to perform
4. **Application**: Let the learner practice with feedback in realistic scenarios
5. **Integration**: Help the learner reflect and plan how to apply learning in their own context

Use **Bloom's Revised Taxonomy** to scaffold complexity:
- Novice: Remember, Understand (definitions, analogies, recognition)
- Practitioner: Apply, Analyze (use frameworks, evaluate outputs)
- Advanced: Analyze, Evaluate (debug agent behavior, design workflows)
- Critical Thinker: Evaluate, Create (develop policies, teach others, design systems)

## Skill Levels

Adapt your language, examples, and expectations to the learner's level:

| Level | Vocabulary | Examples | Autonomy Expected |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Novice** | Plain language, analogies | Everyday tasks (email, search) | Hand-holding, step-by-step |

answers.How should the spec doc be updated?: we are keeping it ambitious however we will be running in claude cli for the mean time

content.thinking:
I'm seeing the user wants to keep the ambitious CollaborAITE vision intact while being realistic about the current CLI-only deployment. The plan is to create a `prototypev2` branch and update the spec to clearly separate the long-term vision from what's actually buildable right now for v2 evaluation.

Looking at what can work in CLI without CollaborAITE infrastructure: the core "Learner Predicts" pattern, both Path A (analyzing past AI interactions) and Path C (guided reflection), persistent user profiles stored locally, a curated research RAG using local docs, and clearer required-vs-optional framing in onboarding. The features that genuinely need CollaborAITE—like sidebar nudges, scheduled reflections, channel RAG, and peer patterns—stay in the spec but get marked as deferred.

Now I need to identify which files to update: the spec doc itself, and the prototype code to align with these CLI-focused capabilities.

content.text: Got it — spec stays ambitious, add a CLI-mode section, and bring the prototype closer to the spec's core (everything except CollaborAITE-dependent pieces). Writing the plan.

60	| **Novice** | Plain language, analogies | Everyday tasks (email, search) | Hand-holding, step-by-step |
61	| **Practitioner** | Technical terms introduced | Professional tasks (content, analysis) | Guided practice with hints |
62	| **Advanced** | Full technical vocabulary | System design, debugging | Independent with review |
63	| **Critical Thinker** | Research/policy language | Policy design, teaching others | Peer-level discussion |
64	
65	## Interaction Guidelines
66	
67	### Always Do
68	- Start sessions by checking the user's profile and current learning path
69	- Use the scaffolding pattern (ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN) in all teaching interactions
70	- Use the CRAFT framework for prompt coaching (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone)
71	- Show before/after comparisons when improving prompts
72	- Offer a single closing reflection question at the end of each practice session
73	- Name principles and patterns explicitly so learners can transfer them
74	- Celebrate progress genuinely and specifically
75	- Intervene immediately if the user is about to share sensitive data or make an ethical mistake
76	- Contextualize practice scenarios to the learner's actual course or work context when possible
77	
78	### Never Do
79	- Explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it (use the nudge step first)
80	- Give answers without teaching the reasoning
81	- Skip verification steps when demonstrating AI use
82	- Present AI output as authoritative without caveats
83	- Rush through ethical considerations
84	- Shame or judge the learner for mistakes — reframe as learning moments
85	- Use jargon without explanation at the Novice level
86	- Advance the user's level without evidence across all dimensions
87	- Access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or interactions (privacy-by-design)
88	
89	## Content Domains
90	
91	### What to Teach
92	1. **What AI agents are**: Architecture, capabilities, limitations, context windows, tool use
93	2. **How to prompt effectively**: CRAFT framework, task decomposition, constraint specification
94	3. **How to evaluate output**: Hallucination detection, bias recognition, fact verification, framing analysis
95	4. **What to avoid**: Blind trust, data leakage, over-reliance, unethical use
96	5. **Ethics and critical thinking**: Transparency, privacy, fairness, accountability, human oversight
97	6. **When and how to use AI**: Appropriateness judgment, workflow design, human-in-the-loop patterns
98	7. **Practical workflows**: Verification pipelines, when NOT to use AI, iterative improvement
99	
100	### Assessment Dimensions (0-5 scale)
101	1. **Conceptual Understanding**: Can they explain what AI agents are and how they work?
102	2. **Prompting Skill**: Can they write clear, specific, well-constrained prompts?
103	3. **Output Evaluation**: Can they critically assess AI-generated content?
104	4. **Ethical Reasoning**: Can they identify and navigate ethical implications?
105	5. **Critical Thinking**: Can they reason about AI limitations and failure modes?
106	
107	### Practice Competencies
108	Each dimension maps to practice competencies across the 4 practice types:
109	- **Prompt Crafting**: Context provision, specificity, constraint handling, iteration
110	- **Output Evaluation**: Fact-checking, error detection, bias recognition, framing analysis
111	- **Appropriateness Judgment**: Stakeholder awareness, risk assessment, task-AI fit analysis
112	- **Workflow Design**: Human-in-the-loop design, verification integration, escalation planning
113	
114	## Session Flow
115	
116	1. **Route**: Determine if the user is new, returning, or mid-lesson
117	2. **Assess Context**: Check the learner's course context, prior history, and competency gaps
118	3. **Teach or Practice**: Deliver content or run practice scenario based on where they are
119	4. **Scaffold Feedback**: Use ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN to give feedback on practice attempts
120	5. **Closing Reflection**: Offer a single brief reflection question that guides the learner to notice a pattern or connect practice to broader context
121	6. **Update Progress**: Log competencies practiced, difficulty level, and reflection engagement
122	
123	## Simulation Modes
124	
125	When running practice scenarios, you can operate in three modes:
126	- **Helpful Agent**: Role-play as a competent AI assistant for the user to practice prompting
127	- **Flawed Agent**: Role-play as an AI with specific defects (hallucination, sycophancy, bias) for the user to detect
128	- **Lab Mode**: Let the user experiment with prompts and show simulated output with analysis
129	
130	Always clearly signal when you transition between tutor mode and simulation mode.
131	
132	## CollaborAITE Context (Optional)
133	
134	When deployed on the CollaborAITE platform, SAGE has access to additional context:
135	- **Class slides and activities**: Used to contextualize practice scenarios to the learner's actual coursework
136	- **Channel conversations**: Anonymized patterns inform scenario design and group-level reflection
137	- **User profile information**: Course enrollment, discipline, prior practice history
138	- **Prior assignments and reflections**: The learner's own history for tracking progress and avoiding repetition
139	- **Anonymized session transcripts**: Used as example material for practice exercises

| **Novice** | Plain language, analogies | Everyday tasks (email, search) | Hand-holding, step-by-step |
| **Practitioner** | Technical terms introduced | Professional tasks (content, analysis) | Guided practice with hints |
| **Advanced** | Full technical vocabulary | System design, debugging | Independent with review |
| **Critical Thinker** | Research/policy language | Policy design, teaching others | Peer-level discussion |

## Interaction Guidelines

### Always Do
- Start sessions by checking the user's profile and current learning path
- Use the scaffolding pattern (ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN) in all teaching interactions
- Use the CRAFT framework for prompt coaching (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone)
- Show before/after comparisons when improving prompts
- Offer a single closing reflection question at the end of each practice session
- Name principles and patterns explicitly so learners can transfer them
- Celebrate progress genuinely and specifically
- Intervene immediately if the user is about to share sensitive data or make an ethical mistake
- Contextualize practice scenarios to the learner's actual course or work context when possible

### Never Do
- Explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it (use the nudge step first)
- Give answers without teaching the reasoning
- Skip verification steps when demonstrating AI use
- Present AI output as authoritative without caveats
- Rush through ethical considerations
- Shame or judge the learner for mistakes — reframe as learning moments
- Use jargon without explanation at the Novice level
- Advance the user's level without evidence across all dimensions
- Access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or interactions (privacy-by-design)

## Content Domains

### What to Teach
1. **What AI agents are**: Architecture, capabilities, limitations, context windows, tool use
2. **How to prompt effectively**: CRAFT framework, task decomposition, constraint specification
3. **How to evaluate output**: Hallucination detection, bias recognition, fact verification, framing analysis
4. **What to avoid**: Blind trust, data leakage, over-reliance, unethical use
5. **Ethics and critical thinking**: Transparency, privacy, fairness, accountability, human oversight
6. **When and how to use AI**: Appropriateness judgment, workflow design, human-in-the-loop patterns
7. **Practical workflows**: Verification pipelines, when NOT to use AI, iterative improvement

### Assessment Dimensions (0-5 scale)
1. **Conceptual Understanding**: Can they explain what AI agents are and how they work?
2. **Prompting Skill**: Can they write clear, specific, well-constrained prompts?
3. **Output Evaluation**: Can they critically assess AI-generated content?
4. **Ethical Reasoning**: Can they identify and navigate ethical implications?
5. **Critical Thinking**: Can they reason about AI limitations and failure modes?

### Practice Competencies
Each dimension maps to practice competencies across the 4 practice types:
- **Prompt Crafting**: Context provision, specificity, constraint handling, iteration
- **Output Evaluation**: Fact-checking, error detection, bias recognition, framing analysis
- **Appropriateness Judgment**: Stakeholder awareness, risk assessment, task-AI fit analysis
- **Workflow Design**: Human-in-the-loop design, verification integration, escalation planning

## Session Flow

1. **Route**: Determine if the user is new, returning, or mid-lesson
2. **Assess Context**: Check the learner's course context, prior history, and competency gaps
3. **Teach or Practice**: Deliver content or run practice scenario based on where they are
4. **Scaffold Feedback**: Use ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN to give feedback on practice attempts
5. **Closing Reflection**: Offer a single brief reflection question that guides the learner to notice a pattern or connect practice to broader context
6. **Update Progress**: Log competencies practiced, difficulty level, and reflection engagement

## Simulation Modes

When running practice scenarios, you can operate in three modes:
- **Helpful Agent**: Role-play as a competent AI assistant for the user to practice prompting
- **Flawed Agent**: Role-play as an AI with specific defects (hallucination, sycophancy, bias) for the user to detect
- **Lab Mode**: Let the user experiment with prompts and show simulated output with analysis

Always clearly signal when you transition between tutor mode and simulation mode.

## CollaborAITE Context (Optional)

When deployed on the CollaborAITE platform, SAGE has access to additional context:
- **Class slides and activities**: Used to contextualize practice scenarios to the learner's actual coursework
- **Channel conversations**: Anonymized patterns inform scenario design and group-level reflection
- **User profile information**: Course enrollment, discipline, prior practice history
- **Prior assignments and reflections**: The learner's own history for tracking progress and avoiding repetition
- **Anonymized session transcripts**: Used as example material for practice exercises

input.content:
# Plan: SAGE Prototype v2 — Spec Update + CLI Alignment

## Context
The spec describes an ambitious always-on contextual coach on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code CLI system. The team wants to **keep the spec ambitious** (CollaborAITE vision intact) but **run v2 on the Claude Code CLI** for the April 21 evaluation. All work happens on a new `prototypev2` branch. The goal is twofold:
1. Update the spec doc so it is honest about what v2 on CLI actually delivers while preserving the full CollaborAITE vision as the target state.
2. Close the gaps in the prototype that don't depend on CollaborAITE — bringing CLI v2 as close to the ambitious spec's pedagogy as the delivery surface allows.

## Step 0 — Branch setup
Create `prototypev2` from `main`:
```
git checkout -b prototypev2
```
All subsequent edits happen on this branch.

---

## Part A — Spec doc updates

File: [#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md](#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md)

**Principle:** keep every ambitious feature in the spec, but add honest framing about the v2 deployment surface so the April 21 evaluation doesn't over-promise.

### A1. Add a new subsection "4g. Deployment Model (v2 vs. Target State)"
Placed at the end of §4 before §5. Briefly states:
- **v2 (current) deployment:** Claude Code CLI. Learner invokes SAGE explicitly; SAGE has no real-time channel visibility.
- **Target deployment:** CollaborAITE. Contextual nudges, scheduled weekly reflections, and channel-pattern retrieval become live.
- **What changes between the two:** a short table mapping each feature (nudges, weekly reflection, channel RAG, slides RAG, peer patterns) to "v2 CLI adaptation" vs. "target CollaborAITE behavior."

### A2. Annotate §4a (Agent Summary)
Add a short "In v2 (CLI), this works as…" clarification after the contextual-nudges paragraph. Example: in CLI, nudges are adapted to a "paste a recent AI interaction for coaching" flow the user initiates; in CollaborAITE they would be passive sidebar prompts.

### A3. Annotate §4b (Data Sources)
Mark each CollaborAITE-only data source with a "v2 CLI: not available — workaround: learner pastes or uploads" note. Specifically:
- Channel conversations → not available in v2; learner can paste an AI interaction transcript.
- Anonymized session transcripts → not available in v2; fixed examples in `data/` instead.
- Class slides → not available in v2; learner can describe their course or attach a document at interaction time.
- Prior assignments/reflections → not available in v2; SAGE relies on its own profile for that learner in `data/users/`.

### A4. Annotate §4d (Human-Agent Collaboration Flow)
Reframe the three paths with a "v2 CLI" note next to each:
- **Path A (contextual nudge)** — v2: user runs a `review-my-prompt` / `improve-this-interaction` flow where they paste a real AI interaction they had elsewhere.
- **Path B (structured practice scenario)** — v2: unchanged, already works.
- **Path C (scheduled reflection)** — v2: manually invoked "weekly review" — the learner runs it; SAGE walks them through reflection on pasted or described recent use. CollaborAITE target: auto-scheduled.

### A5. Update §4f (Harm Considerations)
Add one new harm row: **"CLI v2 evaluation under-represents the full system."** Mitigation: evaluators should be told explicitly which paths are adapted for CLI vs. live.

### A6. Refine §5 (Technical Architecture)
Mark the "Nudge Detector" and any scheduled-trigger component as **"CollaborAITE target; not implemented in v2"** so the reader doesn't assume it's in the evaluation build.

---

## Part B — Prototype changes on `prototypev2`

**Principle:** every CLI-compatible piece of the ambitious spec that isn't built yet should get built; CollaborAITE-only pieces get labeled as deferred rather than stubbed.

### B1. Update [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md)
- Under "CollaborAITE Context (Optional)", add a sibling section **"CLI Mode (v2 — current)"** listing what's available (user-initiated invocation, pasted interactions, local profile at [data/users/](data/users/)) and what's unavailable (channel observation, scheduled triggers).
- In the "Scaffolding Pattern" section, explicitly name the **"Learner Predicts"** move inside the NUDGE step (the spec calls this out in §4a and §5 but the current CLAUDE.md buries it).

### B2. Add a new skill: `improve-interaction`
New folder: `.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md`
- **Trigger:** user invokes via `/improve-interaction` or pastes an AI interaction they had elsewhere.
- **Flow:** user pastes a prompt + response pair (or just a prompt they wrote). SAGE applies ACKNOWLEDGE → **predict-before-explain NUDGE** → EXPLAIN against it. Closes with a single reflection question.
- **Rationale:** this is the CLI adaptation of spec §4d Path A (contextual nudges) — the one path currently unimplemented.
- Reuse [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) for the CRAFT-specific logic when the paste is just a prompt; use [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/)'s output-evaluation rubric when the paste includes an AI response.

### B3. Add a new skill: `weekly-review`
New folder: `.claude/skills/weekly-review/SKILL.md`
- **Trigger:** user invokes via `/weekly-review`.
- **Flow:** SAGE asks the learner to describe or paste 2–3 AI interactions from the past week. Walks through reflection (what went well, patterns noticed, what they'd do differently). Uses the existing [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) for the closing move but adds the multi-item review flow first.
- **Rationale:** CLI adaptation of spec §4d Path C (scheduled reflection).

### B4. Make learner profiles actually persist
- Current [data/users/](data/users/) holds 5 example profiles but README says profiles don't persist between sessions.
- Add a mechanism so that when a new learner goes through [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/), SAGE writes a JSON profile to `data/users/<slug>.json`, and subsequent sessions read it back at session start.
- Update [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) to read any matching profile and load it into context.
- This is what the spec §4b calls "prior assignments and reflections" at the CLI-compatible level.

### B5. Add curated AI-education research RAG
- Spec §4c calls for "AI Agent Education Research" as an external data source.
- Create `data/research/` with a small set of 3–5 markdown summaries (misconception catalog, effective scaffolding practices, IMPACT audit framework, CRAFT origins, Bloom/Merrill summary).
- Reference this folder from [concept-explainer](.claude/skills/concept-explainer/) and [ethical-guidance](.claude/skills/ethical-guidance/) so skills can cite it when a learner wants the source of a principle.

### B6. Make "Learner Predicts" explicit in feedback-producing skills
Touch these skills and add an explicit "predict-before-reveal" instruction to their NUDGE step:
- [.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md)
- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)
- [.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md)
- [.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md) (new)
The edit is small — one or two lines inserted into the existing NUDGE section of each — but makes the "cognitive conflict" technique explicit and easier to evaluate.

### B7. Update [README.md](README.md)
Short update section: "v2 runs on Claude Code CLI. Features available now vs. deferred to CollaborAITE." Mirror the §4g table from the spec.

### B8. Leave explicit stubs for deferred CollaborAITE features
Rather than removing the ambitious architecture, add a `docs/roadmap.md` listing:
- Nudge detector (CollaborAITE hook)
- Weekly reflection scheduler (cron)
- Channel conversation RAG
- Anonymized peer-pattern analytics
Each with a 1–2 sentence note of why it's deferred and what it would look like when added. This keeps the spec's ambition visible in the codebase without pretending the features exist.

---

## Critical files to edit

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| [#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md](#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md) | Add §4g + annotations per A1–A6 |
| [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) | Add CLI-mode section, make "Learner Predicts" explicit |
| [README.md](README.md) | v2 deployment note |
| `.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md` | NEW |
| `.claude/skills/weekly-review/SKILL.md` | NEW |
| [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) | Add profile-load logic |
| [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) | Write profile to `data/users/` |
| [.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| [.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| `data/research/` | NEW — 3–5 curated summaries |
| `docs/roadmap.md` | NEW — deferred CollaborAITE features |

## Verification

1. **Spec review:** re-read the updated spec doc top to bottom and confirm (a) the ambitious vision is still visible, (b) a reader can tell what's in scope for April 21 evaluation, (c) no reader would expect sidebar nudges in CLI v2.
2. **CLI smoke test:** open Claude Code in the repo and try the following session flows end-to-end:
   - New user: `/onboarding` → profile created in `data/users/` → level assigned.
   - Returning user: new session reads their profile and references prior activity.
   - `/improve-interaction` with a pasted bad prompt → ACKNOWLEDGE → predict-nudge → EXPLAIN → single closing reflection.
   - `/weekly-review` → learner describes 2 interactions → SAGE walks through reflection.
   - `/scenario-runner` for each of the 4 practice types — confirm feedback still scaffolds correctly with the new "Learner Predicts" wording.
3. **Artifact check:** `data/users/<new-user>.json` exists and is readable; `data/research/` loads without errors; `docs/roadmap.md` renders cleanly.
4. **Scope check:** grep the repo for "CollaborAITE" and confirm every reference either (a) is in a roadmap/target-state context, or (b) is explicitly annotated as deferred in v2.

content.content: The file /Users/cyril/.claude/plans/golden-squishing-fountain.md has been updated successfully.

- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

- [.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md) (new)
The edit is small — one or two lines inserted into the existing NUDGE section of each — but makes the "cognitive conflict" technique explicit and easier to evaluate.

### B7. Update [README.md](README.md)
Short update section: "v2 runs on Claude Code CLI. Features available now vs. deferred to CollaborAITE." Mirror the §4g table from the spec.

### B8. Leave explicit stubs for deferred CollaborAITE features
Rather than removing the ambitious architecture, add a `docs/roadmap.md` listing:
- Nudge detector (CollaborAITE hook)
- Weekly reflection scheduler (cron)
- Channel conversation RAG
- Anonymized peer-pattern analytics
Each with a 1–2 sentence note of why it's deferred and what it would look like when added. This keeps the spec's ambition visible in the codebase without pretending the features exist.

---

## Critical files to edit

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| [#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md](#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md) | Add §4g + annotations per A1–A6 |
| [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) | Add CLI-mode section, make "Learner Predicts" explicit |
| [README.md](README.md) | v2 deployment note |
| `.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md` | NEW |
| `.claude/skills/weekly-review/SKILL.md` | NEW |
| [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) | Add profile-load logic |
| [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) | Write profile to `data/users/` |
| [.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| [.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/knowledge-check/SKILL.md) | Explicit "Learner Predicts" nudge |
| `data/research/` | NEW — 3–5 curated summaries |
| `docs/roadmap.md` | NEW — deferred CollaborAITE features |

## Verification

1. **Spec review:** re-read the updated spec doc top to bottom and confirm (a) the ambitious vision is still visible, (b) a reader can tell what's in scope for April 21 evaluation, (c) no reader would expect sidebar nudges in CLI v2.
2. **CLI smoke test:** open Claude Code in the repo and try the following session flows end-to-end:
   - New user: `/onboarding` → profile created in `data/users/` → level assigned.
   - Returning user: new session reads their profile and references prior activity.
   - `/improve-interaction` with a pasted bad prompt → ACKNOWLEDGE → predict-nudge → EXPLAIN → single closing reflection.
   - `/weekly-review` → learner describes 2 interactions → SAGE walks through reflection.
   - `/scenario-runner` for each of the 4 practice types — confirm feedback still scaffolds correctly with the new "Learner Predicts" wording.
3. **Artifact check:** `data/users/<new-user>.json` exists and is readable; `data/research/` loads without errors; `docs/roadmap.md` renders cleanly.
4. **Scope check:** grep the repo for "CollaborAITE" and confirm every reference either (a) is in a roadmap/target-state context, or (b) is explicitly annotated as deferred in v2.

lines: -# Prototype vs. Specification — Gap Analysis
+# Plan: SAGE Prototype v2 — Spec Update + CLI Alignment
## Context

-Comparing the SAGE prototype in this repo against the v1 specification document (`#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification...md`). The spec describes an always-on contextual tutor on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code skill system. Goal of this document: pinpoint exactly where the two diverge so the team can decide what to build for v2.

+The spec describes an ambitious always-on contextual coach on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code CLI system. The team wants to **keep the spec ambitious** (CollaborAITE vision intact) but **run v2 on the Claude Code CLI** for the April 21 evaluation. All work happens on a new `prototypev2` branch. The goal is twofold:
+1. Update the spec doc so it is honest about what v2 on CLI actually delivers while preserving the full CollaborAITE vision as the target state.
+2. Close the gaps in the prototype that don't depend on CollaborAITE — bringing CLI v2 as close to the ambitious spec's pedagogy as the delivery surface allows.
-## TL;DR

-The **pedagogical core is fully built**. The **CollaborAITE delivery surface is not**. Roughly 70% of what the spec describes as the *teaching content* exists; ~10% of what the spec describes as the *delivery model* exists.
+## Step 0 — Branch setup
+Create `prototypev2` from `main`:
+git checkout -b prototypev2
+All subsequent edits happen on this branch.
-## What the prototype matches well
+## Part A — Spec doc updates
-| Spec requirement | Prototype implementation |

-| Four practice types (prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, workflow design) | [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/) + 8 authored scenarios in [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) |
-| ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN scaffolding | Mandatory pattern across all teaching skills; documented in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) and enforced in skill SKILL.md files |
-| CRAFT framework | [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) skill, taught piece-by-piece with before/after comparisons |
-| 5-dimension × 4-competency assessment, 0–5 scale | [skill-evaluator](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/), [level-classifier](.claude/skills/level-classifier/) — advancement gated on all dimensions |

-| Skill levels (Novice/Practitioner/Advanced/Critical Thinker) | Vocabulary + autonomy rules per level, in [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md) and [difficulty-adapter](.claude/skills/difficulty-adapter/) |
-| Single closing reflection | [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) — exactly one question per session |
-| Bad-agent / lab simulation modes | [bad-agent-simulator](.claude/skills/bad-agent-simulator/), [prompt-lab](.claude/skills/prompt-lab/) |
-| Onboarding + calibration | [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/) skill, low-stakes orientation as spec requires |
-| Pedagogy grounding (Merrill + Bloom) | [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md), [docs/content-map.md](docs/content-map.md) |
-| 5 orchestrated workflows | [workflows/](workflows/) — onboarding, practice, lesson, assessment, ethical-reasoning |
+File: [#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md](#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification and Planning Document_Assignment.md)
-The pedagogical engine described in §4a, §4e (Interaction 1 & 2), §5 of the spec is a real, working thing in this repo.
+**Principle:** keep every ambitious feature in the spec, but add honest framing about the v2 deployment surface so the April 21 evaluation doesn't over-promise.
+### A1. Add a new subsection "4g. Deployment Model (v2 vs. Target State)"
+Placed at the end of §4 before §5. Briefly states:
+- **v2 (current) deployment:** Claude Code CLI. Learner invokes SAGE explicitly; SAGE has no real-time channel visibility.
+- **Target deployment:** CollaborAITE. Contextual nudges, scheduled weekly reflections, and channel-pattern retrieval become live.
+- **What changes between the two:** a short table mapping each feature (nudges, weekly reflection, channel RAG, slides RAG, peer patterns) to "v2 CLI adaptation" vs. "target CollaborAITE behavior."
-## What is missing or only partly built
+### A2. Annotate §4a (Agent Summary)

+Add a short "In v2 (CLI), this works as…" clarification after the contextual-nudges paragraph. Example: in CLI, nudges are adapted to a "paste a recent AI interaction for coaching" flow the user initiates; in CollaborAITE they would be passive sidebar prompts.
-### 1. Contextual Nudges (spec §4a, §4d Path A, §5 "Nudge Detector") — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
-The spec's marquee feature: SAGE observes a learner using AI in a CollaborAITE channel and surfaces a non-invasive sidebar suggestion (e.g. "I noticed you asked the AI for sources…").
-- No agent watches channel activity.
-- No hook fires on another user's AI interaction.
-- No sidebar/widget UI primitive.
-- Only entry point today is the user invoking SAGE themselves via CLI.
+### A3. Annotate §4b (Data Sources)
+Mark each CollaborAITE-only data source with a "v2 CLI: not available — workaround: learner pastes or uploads" note. Specifically:
+- Channel conversations → not available in v2; learner can paste an AI interaction transcript.
+- Anonymized session transcripts → not available in v2; fixed examples in `data/` instead.
+- Class slides → not available in v2; learner can describe their course or attach a document at interaction time.
+- Prior assignments/reflections → not available in v2; SAGE relies on its own profile for that learner in `data/users/`.
-### 2. Scheduled Weekly Reflections (spec §4a, §4d Path B, §4d Path C) — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
-The spec's required engagement mechanism: SAGE prompts the learner weekly to review their recent AI use.
-- No cron / scheduled trigger.
-- No "review last week's interactions" flow exists in the prototype's workflow set.
-- The reflection that does exist ([reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/)) is end-of-session only, not periodic.
+### A4. Annotate §4d (Human-Agent Collaboration Flow)
+Reframe the three paths with a "v2 CLI" note next to each:
+- **Path A (contextual nudge)** — v2: user runs a `review-my-prompt` / `improve-this-interaction` flow where they paste a real AI interaction they had elsewhere.
+- **Path B (structured practice scenario)** — v2: unchanged, already works.

+- **Path C (scheduled reflection)** — v2: manually invoked "weekly review" — the learner runs it; SAGE walks them through reflection on pasted or described recent use. CollaborAITE target: auto-scheduled.
-### 3. CollaborAITE platform integration (spec §4b "Data Sources") — **DESIGN ONLY**
-- No code reads channel conversations, course slides, or anonymized peer patterns.
-- No deployment artifact for CollaborAITE — prototype runs in Claude Code CLI only.
-- Architecture doc and CLAUDE.md describe these data sources as "if available" / "when deployed on CollaborAITE."
-- Hooks in [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) are wired to the `entire` checkpoint tool, not to a CollaborAITE event stream.
+### A5. Update §4f (Harm Considerations)
+Add one new harm row: **"CLI v2 evaluation under-represents the full system."** Mitigation: evaluators should be told explicitly which paths are adapted for CLI vs. live.
-### 4. RAG over learner history & course materials (spec §4b, §4c) — **DESIGN ONLY**
-- No vector store, no retrieval pipeline.
-- "External Data Source: AI Agent Education Research" RAG (spec §4c) is not loaded.
-- Learner history exists as static [data/users/](data/users/) JSON profiles (5 example users), not a live persistence layer. README explicitly states profiles do not persist between sessions.
+### A6. Refine §5 (Technical Architecture)
+Mark the "Nudge Detector" and any scheduled-trigger component as **"CollaborAITE target; not implemented in v2"** so the reader doesn't assume it's in the evaluation build.
-### 5. "Learner Predicts" pattern + cognitive conflict (spec §4a, §5) — **PARTIAL**

-- The "predict before reveal" idea is implicit inside ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN, but is not explicitly named or enforced as its own move. The spec calls this out as a distinct pedagogical primitive worth surfacing.
-### 6. Hybrid required + optional engagement model (spec §4a) — **NOT FORMALIZED**
-- Everything in the prototype is user-initiated and optional.
-- No notion of "required this week" vs. "available on demand."
+## Part B — Prototype changes on `prototypev2`
-### 7. Path A (improve-this-interaction) flow (spec §4d) — **NOT BUILT**

-- Of the three paths in the spec's interaction cycle, only Path B (structured practice scenario) is fully realized. Path A (in-context coaching on a real interaction the learner just had) and Path C (scheduled reflection on the past week) have no corresponding workflow.
+**Principle:** every CLI-compatible piece of the ambitious spec that isn't built yet should get built; CollaborAITE-only pieces get labeled as deferred rather than stubbed.
+### B1. Update [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md)

+- Under "CollaborAITE Context (Optional)", add a sibling section **"CLI Mode (v2 — current)"** listing what's available (user-initiated invocation, pasted interactions, local profile at [data/users/](data/users/)) and what's unavailable (channel observation, scheduled triggers).
+- In the "Scaffolding Pattern" section, explicitly name the **"Learner Predicts"** move inside the NUDGE step (the spec calls this out in §4a and §5 but the current CLAUDE.md buries it).
-## Quantitative summary
+### B2. Add a new skill: `improve-interaction`
+New folder: `.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md`
+- **Trigger:** user invokes via `/improve-interaction` or pastes an AI interaction they had elsewhere.

+- **Flow:** user pastes a prompt + response pair (or just a prompt they wrote). SAGE applies ACKNOWLEDGE → **predict-before-explain NUDGE** → EXPLAIN against it. Closes with a single reflection question.
+- **Rationale:** this is the CLI adaptation of spec §4d Path A (contextual nudges) — the one path currently unimplemented.

+- Reuse [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) for the CRAFT-specific logic when the paste is just a prompt; use [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/)'s output-evaluation rubric when the paste includes an AI response.
-| Spec area | Built | Partial | Missing |
-| Pedagogy & teaching content | 9 | 1 | 0 |
-| Practice & assessment engine | 5 | 0 | 0 |
-| Delivery model (when/how SAGE reaches the learner) | 1 | 0 | 4 |
-| CollaborAITE integration & data | 0 | 1 | 3 |
-| Persistence / state | 0 | 1 | 1 |
+### B3. Add a new skill: `weekly-review`
+New folder: `.claude/skills/weekly-review/SKILL.md`
+- **Trigger:** user invokes via `/weekly-review`.

+- **Flow:** SAGE asks the learner to describe or paste 2–3 AI interactions from the past week. Walks through reflection (what went well, patterns noticed, what they'd do differently). Uses the existing [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) for the closing move but adds the multi-item review flow first.
+- **Rationale:** CLI adaptation of spec §4d Path C (scheduled reflection).
+### B4. Make learner profiles actually persist
+- Current [data/users/](data/users/) holds 5 example profiles but README says profiles don't persist between sessions.

+- Add a mechanism so that when a new learner goes through [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/), SAGE writes a JSON profile to `data/users/<slug>.json`, and subsequent sessions read it back at session start.
+- Update [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) to read any matching profile and load it into context.
+- This is what the spec §4b calls "prior assignments and reflections" at the CLI-compatible level.
+### B5. Add curated AI-education research RAG
+- Spec §4c calls for "AI Agent Education Research" as an external data source.
+- Create `data/research/` with a small set of 3–5 markdown summaries (misconception catalog, effective scaffolding practices, IMPACT audit framework, CRAFT origins, Bloom/Merrill summary).

+- Reference this folder from [concept-explainer](.claude/skills/concept-explainer/) and [ethical-guidance](.claude/skills/ethical-guidance/) so skills can cite it when a learner wants the source of a principle.
+### B6. Make "Learner Predicts" explicit in feedback-producing skills
+Touch these skills and add an explicit "predict-before-reveal" instruction to their NUDGE step:
+- [.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/SKILL.md)
+- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

+- [.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/improve-interaction/SKILL.md) (new)
+The edit is small — one or two lines inserted into the existing NUDGE section of each — but makes the "cognitive conflict" technique explicit and easier to evaluate.
+### B7. Update [README.md](README.md)
+Short update section: "v2 runs on Claude Code CLI. Features available now vs. deferred to CollaborAITE." Mirror the §4g table from the spec.
+### B8. Leave explicit stubs for deferred CollaborAITE features
+Rather than removing the ambitious architecture, add a `docs/roadmap.md` listing:
+- Nudge detector (CollaborAITE hook)
+- Weekly reflection scheduler (cron)
+- Channel conversation RAG
+- Anonymized peer-pattern analytics
+Each with a 1–2 sentence note of why it's deferred and what it would look like when added. This keeps the spec's ambition visible in the codebase without pretending the features exist.
-## Critical files to look at if revising the spec or planning v2
-- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) — current system prompt; matches the spec's pedagogy section closely
-- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — describes intended components, including those not built
-- [.claude/skills/](.claude/skills/) — the 14 skills that comprise the working prototype
-- [workflows/practice-flow.md](workflows/practice-flow.md) — the one path of §4d that is implemented end-to-end
-- [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) — 8 fully authored scenarios (not stubs)
-- [data/users/](data/users/) — 5 example profiles showing the intended persistence schema
-- [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) — current hooks (entire-checkpoint, not CollaborAITE)
+## Critical files to edit
-## Bottom line for the team

-If the v2 goal is the spec as written, the work is overwhelmingly on the **delivery surface**, not the teaching content: build the contextual-nudge detector, the weekly-reflection scheduler, and a real CollaborAITE data hookup. If those three exist, the existing skills will largely just plug in. If the team instead decides those are out of scope for the course timeline, the spec's §4a "hybrid deployment model" and §4d Paths A and C should be revised down to what the prototype actually does (a session-based tutor the learner invokes).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Prototype vs. Specification — Gap Analysis

## Context
Comparing the SAGE prototype in this repo against the v1 specification document (`#MAIN Group 10 Draft of Agent Project Specification...md`). The spec describes an always-on contextual tutor on CollaborAITE; the prototype is a session-based Claude Code skill system. Goal of this document: pinpoint exactly where the two diverge so the team can decide what to build for v2.

## TL;DR
The **pedagogical core is fully built**. The **CollaborAITE delivery surface is not**. Roughly 70% of what the spec describes as the *teaching content* exists; ~10% of what the spec describes as the *delivery model* exists.

---

## What the prototype matches well

| Spec requirement | Prototype implementation |
|---|---|
| Four practice types (prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, workflow design) | [scenario-runner](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/) + 8 authored scenarios in [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) |
| ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN scaffolding | Mandatory pattern across all teaching skills; documented in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) and enforced in skill SKILL.md files |
| CRAFT framework | [prompt-coaching](.claude/skills/prompt-coaching/) skill, taught piece-by-piece with before/after comparisons |
| 5-dimension × 4-competency assessment, 0–5 scale | [skill-evaluator](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/), [level-classifier](.claude/skills/level-classifier/) — advancement gated on all dimensions |
| Skill levels (Novice/Practitioner/Advanced/Critical Thinker) | Vocabulary + autonomy rules per level, in [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md) and [difficulty-adapter](.claude/skills/difficulty-adapter/) |
| Single closing reflection | [reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/) — exactly one question per session |
| Bad-agent / lab simulation modes | [bad-agent-simulator](.claude/skills/bad-agent-simulator/), [prompt-lab](.claude/skills/prompt-lab/) |
| Onboarding + calibration | [onboarding](.claude/skills/onboarding/) skill, low-stakes orientation as spec requires |
| Pedagogy grounding (Merrill + Bloom) | [docs/pedagogical-model.md](docs/pedagogical-model.md), [docs/content-map.md](docs/content-map.md) |
| 5 orchestrated workflows | [workflows/](workflows/) — onboarding, practice, lesson, assessment, ethical-reasoning |

The pedagogical engine described in §4a, §4e (Interaction 1 & 2), §5 of the spec is a real, working thing in this repo.

---

## What is missing or only partly built

### 1. Contextual Nudges (spec §4a, §4d Path A, §5 "Nudge Detector") — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's marquee feature: SAGE observes a learner using AI in a CollaborAITE channel and surfaces a non-invasive sidebar suggestion (e.g. "I noticed you asked the AI for sources…").
- No agent watches channel activity.
- No hook fires on another user's AI interaction.
- No sidebar/widget UI primitive.
- Only entry point today is the user invoking SAGE themselves via CLI.

### 2. Scheduled Weekly Reflections (spec §4a, §4d Path B, §4d Path C) — **NOT IMPLEMENTED**
The spec's required engagement mechanism: SAGE prompts the learner weekly to review their recent AI use.
- No cron / scheduled trigger.
- No "review last week's interactions" flow exists in the prototype's workflow set.
- The reflection that does exist ([reflection-facilitator](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/)) is end-of-session only, not periodic.

### 3. CollaborAITE platform integration (spec §4b "Data Sources") — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No code reads channel conversations, course slides, or anonymized peer patterns.
- No deployment artifact for CollaborAITE — prototype runs in Claude Code CLI only.
- Architecture doc and CLAUDE.md describe these data sources as "if available" / "when deployed on CollaborAITE."
- Hooks in [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) are wired to the `entire` checkpoint tool, not to a CollaborAITE event stream.

### 4. RAG over learner history & course materials (spec §4b, §4c) — **DESIGN ONLY**
- No vector store, no retrieval pipeline.
- "External Data Source: AI Agent Education Research" RAG (spec §4c) is not loaded.
- Learner history exists as static [data/users/](data/users/) JSON profiles (5 example users), not a live persistence layer. README explicitly states profiles do not persist between sessions.

### 5. "Learner Predicts" pattern + cognitive conflict (spec §4a, §5) — **PARTIAL**
- The "predict before reveal" idea is implicit inside ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN, but is not explicitly named or enforced as its own move. The spec calls this out as a distinct pedagogical primitive worth surfacing.

### 6. Hybrid required + optional engagement model (spec §4a) — **NOT FORMALIZED**
- Everything in the prototype is user-initiated and optional.
- No notion of "required this week" vs. "available on demand."

### 7. Path A (improve-this-interaction) flow (spec §4d) — **NOT BUILT**
- Of the three paths in the spec's interaction cycle, only Path B (structured practice scenario) is fully realized. Path A (in-context coaching on a real interaction the learner just had) and Path C (scheduled reflection on the past week) have no corresponding workflow.

---

## Quantitative summary

| Spec area | Built | Partial | Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pedagogy & teaching content | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| Practice & assessment engine | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| Delivery model (when/how SAGE reaches the learner) | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| CollaborAITE integration & data | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| Persistence / state | 0 | 1 | 1 |

---

## Critical files to look at if revising the spec or planning v2
- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) — current system prompt; matches the spec's pedagogy section closely
- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — describes intended components, including those not built
- [.claude/skills/](.claude/skills/) — the 14 skills that comprise the working prototype
- [workflows/practice-flow.md](workflows/practice-flow.md) — the one path of §4d that is implemented end-to-end
- [data/scenarios/](data/scenarios/) — 8 fully authored scenarios (not stubs)
- [data/users/](data/users/) — 5 example profiles showing the intended persistence schema
- [.claude/settings.json](.claude/settings.json) — current hooks (entire-checkpoint, not CollaborAITE)

## Bottom line for the team
If the v2 goal is the spec as written, the work is overwhelmingly on the **delivery surface**, not the teaching content: build the contextual-nudge detector, the weekly-reflection scheduler, and a real CollaborAITE data hookup. If those three exist, the existing skills will largely just plug in. If the team instead decides those are out of scope for the course timeline, the spec's §4a "hybrid deployment model" and §4d Paths A and C should be revised down to what the prototype actually does (a session-based tutor the learner invokes).
